title: Cat Hind Leg Stiffness After Sleeping: Causes & When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Noticing your cat is stiff when waking up? Learn about common causes like arthritis and when you should consult a veterinarian for care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Stiffness in a cat's hind legs is often a sign of osteoarthritis, which is manageable through veterinary care, though a sudden loss of mobility requires immediate emergency attention.

What causes my cat to have stiff hind legs after resting?

Stiffness when waking up or "warming up" after a nap is often a primary sign of degenerative joint disease, commonly known as osteoarthritis. As cats age, the cartilage that cushions their joints can wear down, leading to inflammation and discomfort that is most noticeable after periods of rest. While many owners mistake this for simple aging, it is actually a manageable medical condition that affects mobility.

Is my cat’s hind leg stiffness a veterinary emergency?

Urgency Level: Low.

  • If your cat is still eating, drinking, and able to move around (even if slowly), this is not an immediate emergency; schedule a routine appointment within the next week.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if your cat suddenly loses the ability to use their back legs.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if your cat appears to be in extreme distress or has cold paws, as these may be signs of a serious vascular issue.

How can taking a video of my cat's movement help with a diagnosis?

  • Because cats often hide pain at the veterinary clinic due to adrenaline, home videos provide a more accurate representation of their discomfort.
  • Capture a video of your cat waking up and walking in their natural environment to show the vet their true mobility.
  • A video allows your vet to analyze the specific gait and the degree of stiffness.
  • Visual evidence helps your veterinarian identify which joints are most affected, leading to a faster and more accurate diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Hindlimb stiffness in cats after sleeping may be related to pain or mechanical causes. Pain-related lameness can manifest as weight-bearing or non-weight-bearing deficits. One potential cause of hindlimb stiffness is arterial thromboembolism, where the gastrocnemius muscles are often firm, and the cat may or may not be able to move the legs above the stifles. Diagnosis of lameness requires correctly determining the cause, as treatment varies greatly depending on the underlying issue. If lameness persists or is accompanied by other clinical signs, veterinary consultation is warranted for proper diagnosis, which may involve physical examination and diagnostic imaging.

Chapter: Neurology, Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1096)

faqs.question: Is it normal for senior cats to be stiff?
faqs.answer: While common, stiffness is not a 'normal' part of aging that should be ignored; it is usually a sign of chronic pain from arthritis that can be treated.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain relievers for stiffness?
faqs.answer: No. Never give human medications like aspirin or ibuprofen to a cat, as they are highly toxic. Only use medications prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: What are the signs of joint pain in cats?
faqs.answer: Besides stiffness, you may notice your cat hesitating to jump, grooming less, or becoming more irritable when touched near their hips or back.
